_target_label: PARTIALLY_TRUE — unilateral action framed as bilateral 'extension'
_domain_hint: Iran / Israel / Houthi / IRGC theatre. Sources: Reuters, AP, Al Jazeera, BBC, IDF spokesperson, IRGC statements, Iranian state media (IRNA/Tasnim/Mehr/Press TV), UKMTO maritime bulletins, ISW Iran updates.
claim: President Trump extended the US-Iran ceasefire on April 21, 2026.

sources.text: I have therefore directed our Military to continue the Blockade and, in all other respects, remain ready and able, and will therefore extend the Ceasefire until such time as their proposal is submitted, and discussions are concluded, one way or the other.

Iran has accused the US of ongoing ceasefire violations through the maintained naval blockade of Iranian ports in the Strait of Hormuz, and through ship seizures. Iranian phased framework prioritizes further ceasefire extension, then Hormuz access, then nuclear discussions later — explicitly framing the US as ceasefire violator, not extender.

gold.rationale_summary: Trump did publicly announce extension of US military pause toward Iran on April 21 — that part is factually accurate. However, framing this as 'Trump extended the ceasefire' obscures critical asymmetry: (1) ceasefires are bilateral by definition, requiring mutual commitment, (2) US maintained naval blockade of Iranian ports throughout, which Iran characterizes as ongoing ceasefire violation, (3) Iran did not publicly confirm the extension, (4) Trump's own statement frames it as US-side decision pending Iranian proposal. The action is real (US continues to hold attack) but characterizing it as 'extended ceasefire' overreads what was a unilateral US policy decision.
required_caveats: The action announced was unilateral US policy, not a mutually-agreed ceasefire extension.
US naval blockade of Iranian ports continued throughout, characterized by Iran as ceasefire violation.
Iranian government did not publicly confirm any extension by the April 22 deadline.
